data.title: Harbor Small Cap Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
With 2026 underway, the market appears poised for a shift toward broader participation and more fundamental drivers of return. - Westfield Capital Management Company, L.P.

Market In Review

Equity markets posted modest gains during the fourth quarter of 2025, capping a year marked by early trade shocks, robust earnings, and a growing divergence between headline indexes and underlying breadth. While large-cap technology names remained a focal point, the final months of 2025 saw renewed strength in small caps and cyclical sectors, driven by resilient consumer spending, a steepening yield curve, and continued disinflation. The Federal Reserve's ("Fed") dovish pivot and easing financial conditions further supported sentiment — even as investors grew more discerning about AI-driven growth — and labor market dynamics remained in flux.

Portfolio Performance

During the fourth quarter, the Harbor Small Cap Growth Fund (Institutional Class, "Fund") returned 3.09%, outperforming the Russell 2000® Growth Index ("Index"), which returned 1.22%.

From a sector perspective, strength within Industrials and Information Technology ("IT") offset relative weakness in Health Care. The Fund benefited from both positive stock-specific return and a common factor tailwind during the quarter. In particular, the Fund's overweight exposure to size offset a headwind from an underweight exposure to volatility and momentum.

Contributors & Detractors

During the quarter, Revolution Medicines (RVMD), a clinical-stage, oncology-focused biotech company with promising drugs designed to address previously undruggable large markets such as pancreatic cancer was a top contributor to relative performance, as confidence continued to build around its platform. Shares advanced as regulatory validation further derisked daraxonrasib and reinforced its potential across multiple rat sarcoma ("RAS")-driven cancers. Investor recognition improved as the breadth of Revolution's pipeline and size of the opportunity in historically undruggable mutations became more apparent. In early 2026, reports that AbbVie (ABBV) is in advanced acquisition talks with the company highlighted the platform's strategic value, in our opinion. With RAS mutations implicated in roughly 30% of new cancer diagnoses and daraxonrasib holding Breakthrough Therapy Designation, we believe Revolution remains a high-upside oncology platform. We did not make any adjustments to the position during the quarter.

Comfort Systems USA (FIX), a provider of mechanical and electrical contracting services, was among the top contributing names during the quarter, as investor preference shifted toward industrial services companies with durable secular growth exposure. Shares benefited from increasing recognition of the company's positioning in data centers, advanced manufacturing, and complex HVAC systems. The October earnings report reinforced confidence in Comfort Systems' ability to convert a strong backlog and increasingly sophisticated project mix into sustained earnings growth. As execution consistency and mix quality continue to improve, the market has begun to view the business less as a cyclical contractor and more as a high-quality industrial compounder with both growth and defensive characteristics, in our opinion. We trimmed the position during the quarter.

FTAI Aviation (FTAI), an aviation services company transitioning from asset leasing to engine maintenance and parts monetization, was among the top contributing names during the period, as investor focus shifted toward its higher-quality business mix. Shares benefited from growing confidence in the company's pivot toward engine maintenance repair and overhaul and aftermarket parts, which offer greater earnings visibility, the potential for stronger cash generation, and more durable margins than traditional leasing. This evolution differentiated FTAI from cyclical peers and supported a rerating narrative. With increasing exposure to narrow-body engines, such as the CFM56 and V2500, which power a large portion of the global fleet, FTAI is building the potential for a recurring, higher-margin revenue stream with attractive long-term growth characteristics. We did not make any adjustments to the position during the quarter.

Abivax (ABVX), a clinical stage biotech company focused on chronic inflammatory diseases, such as ulcerative colitis ("UC") and Crohn's disease, was a new addition to the Fund during the quarter. We trimmed our position on strength but maintain conviction in the thesis. With a large unmet market in UC and potential expansion into Crohn's disease (late stage trial initiating) and other indications, we believe Abivax is positioned for a major inflection as data and regulatory timelines unfold.

Vaxcyte (PCVX), a novel vaccine developer, which had been under pressure earlier in the year due to confusion over its Phase 2 trial for its VAX-24 in infants, which was compounded by concerns about the potential impact of the political administration and FDA leadership on vaccine companies, recovered some of the prior losses on improved sentiment during the quarter. We did not make any adjustments to the position and continue to believe that the market opportunity remains robust. We are optimistic about the company's catalyst path in 2026.

During the quarter, Commvault Systems (CVLT), a provider of data protection and cyber resilience software, detracted from performance, as continued multiple compression weighed on the stock. Despite steady execution and progress in its transition toward a higher-growth, subscription-based cyber resilience platform, market sentiment failed to reflect improving fundamentals. The company continues to benefit from strong, annual recurring revenue growth, cloud-driven demand, and expanding free-cash-flow margins, but valuation pressure persisted amid broader risk aversion toward mid-cap software. While the long-term thesis remains intact, the stock's near-term performance lagged, as investors remained cautious despite improving business quality. We did not make any adjustments to the position during the quarter.

Legend Biotech (LEGN), a biopharmaceutical company specializing in innovative cell therapies for oncology, was among the bottom-contributing names during the period. The stock underperformed due to what we believe was an overreaction to data from a competitor, despite maintaining a strong competitive position in multiple myeloma and a credible path toward earlier line treatment adoption. With its lead asset well positioned relative to competitors and a pathway toward profitability expected by 2026, we added to our position during the quarter.

Houlihan Lokey (HLI), a boutique investment bank, had a strong earnings report during the quarter, with better-than-expected revenues, tax rate, and non-compensation expenses. However, the stock sold off, as sentiment for the group waivered amid a tough earnings season for a number of boutique peers. We took advantage of the dislocation to add to our position during the quarter.

WillScot Holdings (WSC), a provider of modular and portable storage services, underperformed amid a weakening non-residential cycle, even with strong peers like URI breaking down and its largest competitor signaling another tough year ahead. The stock triggered our downside flag system, and we decided to exit the position during the quarter, as we concluded that the potential upside from rate cuts or a non-residential rebound is too uncertain, compounded by management turnover, which has hurt confidence.

Glaukos (GKOS), a med-tech company specializing in innovative treatments for glaucoma, sold off in October after the announcement that a Carrier Advisory Committee meeting was scheduled with Medicare contractors in November. This raised investors' fears that the meeting could be a forum to discuss potential reimbursement changes for the company's iDose drug therapy. We had identified pricing as potentially impeding the iDose launch as the primary risk to our thesis, and our doctor checks weren't suggesting a recovery in the near term, so we decided to exit the position. Following our sale, Glaukos reported a strong third quarter, and we faced a headwind from not owning the stock.

Buys & Sells

We benefited from M&A activity in the Health Care sector and sold our positions in Merus (MRUS), a clinical-stage immuno-oncology company focused on bispecific antibody therapeutics, and 89Bio (ETNB), a clinical-stage biopharma company focused on cardiometabolic disorders, following the announcements that Genmab (GMAB) and Roche (RHHBY), respectively, would be acquiring the companies.

We sold our position in Rocket Lab (RKLB), a space technology company specializing in satellite launch services and advanced space systems. Rocket Lab outperformed following the announcement that the company was acquiring Geost, which fits with its strategy of being a one-stop-shop for space launch and space products for customers.

We sold our position in IT infrastructure outsourcing company, Kyndryl Holdings (KD), during the quarter. The stock outperformed in the first half of the year, and we had been trimming our position on strength before the stock sold off sharply on a small revenue miss, despite in-line earnings per share and guidance. The stock triggered our downside flag system, and we decided to exit the position given broader IT services headwinds.

We initiated positions in Avidity Bioscience (RNA), a clinical-stage biopharma company focused on RNA therapies for rare muscle diseases, and Abivax SA (ABVX), a clinical-stage biopharma company focused on chronic inflammatory diseases.

We added exposure to beneficiaries of AI and data center growth with the purchase of Legence, a mechanical contractor for mission-critical facilities such as data centers, life sciences labs, and hospitals. Legence benefits from secular growth tailwinds including AI/data center build outs, decarbonization, and government incentives like the U.S. infrastructure/energy transition agenda.

We bought MKS (MKSI), a semi-cap equipment company with exposure to NAND recovery in memory, which could be a beneficiary of tailwinds from AI.

Overweights & Underweights

Financials represented the Fund's largest overweight relative to the Index at the end of 2024. We continue to be overweight the sector, but as of December 31, 2025, the Industrials sector represented the largest overweight relative to the Index. Within Industrials, the overweight was primarily driven by AI-tied names, but we are also finding attractive investment opportunities in more cyclically oriented names. The current industrial recession is emerging from one of the longest downturns in modern history. With Fed easing, stimulus measures, and leading indicators turning higher, we believe cyclicals are set up for a recovery into 2026. The combination of rate cuts and reversion to the mean creates catch-up potential.

Consumer Staples represented the Fund's largest underweight at the end of 2024 and continues to be a relative underweight as of December 31, 2025, as we believe there are more compelling investment opportunities with more attractive growth profiles in other sectors. With that said, as of year-end, the Materials sector is the Fund's largest underweight relative to the Index, where our exposure is limited to one holding within Chemicals: Avient Corp.

Outlook

With 2026 underway, the market appears poised for a shift toward broader participation and more fundamental drivers of return. Monetary policy is likely to remain supportive, and earnings expectations suggest a more balanced contribution across sectors and size segments. However, persistent macro uncertainties – including labor softness, elevated valuations, and episodic volatility – underscore the importance of maintaining a selective and risk-aware approach. In our view, a focus on high-quality businesses with improving fundamentals and valuation support will remain essential in this evolving environment.

Harbor Small Cap Growth (HASGX) Fund (HASGX) Quarterly Commentary & Attribution

Subadvisor (since 11/01/2000): Westfield Capital Management Company, L.P.

Risks

There is no guarantee that the investment objective of the Fund will be achieved. Stock markets are volatile and equity values can decline significantly in response to adverse issuer, political, regulatory, market and economic conditions. Stocks of small cap companies pose special risks, including possible illiquidity and greater price volatility than stocks of larger, more established companies.

Benchmarks

The Russell 2000® Growth Index is an unmanaged index representing the smallest 2000 stocks with the highest price-to-book ratio and future earnings. This unmanaged index does not reflect fees and expenses and is not available for direct investment. The Russell 2000® Growth Index and Russell® are trademarks of Frank Russell Company.

Disclosures

All holdings-related data is provided by FactSet. Because FactSet relies on external sources for its data, that data may differ slightly from actual values maintained by Harbor Funds.

Due to the security valuation procedures of the Fund and intra-day trading activity not included in the FactSet calculations, the actual returns may vary. From time to time the cash return in the portfolio may appear distorted based on the way FactSet's attribution calculation methodology addresses delayed settlements.

Best and Worst Performers sections reflect stocks in the portfolio for the quarter with an average weight of 0.25% or greater.

Views expressed herein are drawn from commentary provided to Harbor by the subadvisor and may not be reflective of their current opinions or future actions, are subject to change without prior notice, and should not be considered investment advice.

This information should not be considered as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. The weightings, holdings, industries, sectors, countries, and returns mentioned may change at any time and may not represent current or future investments.

As a result of changing market conditions, expenses and other statistics may change at any time and may differ from those shown.

Sector allocations are determined using the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS), which is a service of Morgan Stanley Capital International (MSCI) and Standard & Poor's (S&P).

Active Currency Contribution is the Currency Contribution of the portfolio minus the Currency Contribution of the benchmark.

Allocation Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return that is attributable to taking different group bets from the benchmark. (If either the portfolio or the benchmark has no position in a given group, allocation effect is the lone effect.) A group's allocation effect equals the average percent capitalization of the portfolio's group minus the average percent cap of the benchmark's group times the total return of the benchmark group minus the total return of the benchmark.

Average Weight is the dollar value (price times the shares held) of the security or group, divided by the total dollar value of the entire portfolio displayed as a percentage. It is calculated as the simple arithmetic average of daily values.

Contribution to Return is the contribution of a security or group to the overall portfolio return. It is calculated as the security weight multiplied by the daily security return linked daily across the reporting period.

Currency Contribution is Total Return in USD subtracting out the Local Returns.

Local Returns are the Total Return of the portfolio or benchmark using the local currency.

Selection Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return attributable to choosing different securities within groups from the benchmark. A group's security selection effect equals the average weight of the benchmark's group times the total return of the portfolio's group minus the total return of the benchmark's group.

Total Effect is the sum of Allocation Effect and Selection Effect. The total effect represents the opportunity cost of what was done in a group relative to the overall portfolio. It is not just the difference between percent contribution in the portfolio and benchmark. At the overall portfolio level, the two numbers are equal. At the group level, they can be different.

Total Return is the price change of a security or group including dividends accrued over the report period (or the in-portfolio return) which includes only the time period that each security was in the portfolio.

Retirement Class shares commenced operations on March 1, 2016. The performance attributed to the Retirement Class shares prior to that date is that of the Institutional Class shares. Performance prior to March 1, 2016 has not been adjusted to reflect the lower expenses of Retirement Class shares. During this period, Retirement Class shares would have had returns similar to, but somewhat higher than, Institutional Class shares due to the fact that Retirement Class shares represent interests in the same portfolio as Institutional Class shares but are subject to lower expenses.Expense ratio information is as of the Fund's current prospectus, as supplemented. data.title: Top 5 Mining Stocks To Watch In 2026: No. 1 - Troilus Mining
data.description:
The year 2026 is here, and it is time for the traditional "Top 5 Mining Stocks to Watch" list. 2025 was a huge success, as the record-breaking metals prices pushed the featured companies to triple-digit returns. As of December 29, Aris Mining (ARMN) was up 360%, followed by Taseko Mines (TGB) (186%), and Eldorado Gold (EGO) (141%). In the case of Calibre Mining, the calculation is a little more complex, as in June, the company was acquired by Equinox Gold (EQX). However, taking into account the 2024 closing price of Calibre, and the current value of 0.31 Equinox shares, Calibre is technically nearly 102% up. The least successful pick was Franco-Nevada (FNV), although a 78% gain can hardly be taken as a failure. On average, the 2025 Top 5 list gained 191.4%, the VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ) (169%), the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) (154%), the SPDR S&P Metals and Mining ETF (XME) (86%), and also the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) (17%).

It is not realistic to expect similar gains also in 2026, as the gold, silver, and copper prices will most probably ease the pace of growth (and possibly even give up a portion of the 2025 gains), however, numerous mining companies expect some major catalysts and have a good probability of outperforming their peers. Just like every year, I know that there are definitely some other companies with high potential that could have been considered for inclusion in the Top 5. If you are missing such a company on the list, please feel free to mention it in the comments section.

The fifth place on the 2026 list was captured by NexGold Mining (NXGCF), the fourth place was captured by U.S. Gold Corp. (USAU), the third place was captured by Taseko Mines, and the second one by Aris Mining (ARMN). And the No. 1 for 2026 is Troilus Mining (CHXMF). Troilus is not in production and it hasn't even started the mine construction yet. However, it is developing a large brownfield project, it expects several major catalysts this year, and it is deeply undervalued. If everything goes well, Troilus is aimed to deliver sizeable returns in 2026, and much more over the next 1-2 years. Triple-digit gains should be achievable quite easily if everything goes well.

The Current State of Troilus Mining

Troilus Mining's cornerstone asset is the Troilus gold project, located in the Val-d'Or district of Quebec, on the Frotet-Evans Greenstone Belt. It is a brownfield project, as the old mine was in production between 1996 and 2010. According to Troilus, infrastructure and facilities worth $500 million are still in place, which helped to reduce the projected construction CAPEX. Unfortunately, even with the savings, the CAPEX is projected at $1.08 billion.

According to the 2023 resource estimate, the deposit contains inferred and indicated resources of more than 10.8 million toz gold and 845 million lb copper, or 13 million toz of gold equivalent. As much as 7.26 million toz of gold equivalent is contained in the reserves.

According to the feasibility study, the reserves should be sufficient for 22 years of mining operations. The mine should have a throughput rate of 50,0000 tpd. The average annual production should amount to 303,000 toz of gold equivalent (244,600 toz gold & 17.3 million lb copper & 446,700 toz silver), at an AISC of $1,109/toz gold. At a base case gold price of $1,975/toz, copper price of $4.05/lb, and silver price of $23/toz, the after-tax NPV (5%) equals $885 million, and the after-tax IRR equals 14%.

The open-pit dewatering is underway, and so is the permitting process. Troilus expects the financing package to be completed in Q1 2026, and the final permits to be granted in H2 2026. If there are no negative surprises, the construction should start in 2027, with the first gold production in 2028.

The 2026 Catalysts

As mentioned above, two major catalysts are expected in 2026. The first one is the completion of the financing package. The negotiations are well underway, and they should be completed in Q1. The plan is to finance as much as $1 billion via a debt. The process started in 2024, when a syndicate of institutions including Societe Generale, KfW IPEX-Bank, and Export Development Canada was mandated to secure a debt financing of up to $0.7 billion. However, following the positive gold price development and significantly improved economics and attractiveness of the project, Troilus increased the mandate to $1 billion. Obtaining a $1 billion debt financing would be a huge success and major positive catalyst, as it would mean a strong sign of confidence in the project, and it would reduce the financing and share dilution risks significantly.

The second major catalyst is expected in H2 2026, when the permitting process should be completed. This will be a major catalyst, as by that time, the construction financing should be secured, therefore, the main construction activities should start shortly thereafter. Probably in early 2027.

Both of these two events will derisk the project significantly, and they should push the share price significantly higher, because the current gap between the market capitalisation of the company and the NPV of the Troilus project is really huge. At the base-case metals prices of $1,975/toz gold and $4.05/lb copper, the after-tax NPV (5%) amounts to $885 million. It is slightly more than the current market capitalisation of $870 million. But the current market value of gold is not $1,975/toz. It is over $5,300/toz. Based on the sensitivity analysis data, at a gold price of $3,000/toz, the after-tax NPV (5%) is approximately $2.8 billion; at a gold price of $4,000/toz, the value grows to $4.7 billion, and at a gold price of $5,000/toz, it is more than $6.5 billion.

Further catalysts may be provided by the exploration activities. Troilus has identified numerous regional exploration targets on the property that covers an area of 435 km². Moreover, high-grade zones have also been identified inside the resource area and nearby. Further positive drill results may provide some more upside.

Conclusion

Of course, besides the significant upside potential, there are also risks. First of all, there are the gold, and, to a smaller extent, also copper prices. The gold prices are over $5,300/toz. A correction is inevitable; however, it is only to be seen when it comes, at what price it comes, and where it stops. Unfortunately, any more meaningful decline in gold prices will weigh on the gold miners and developers, Troilus included, negatively. The good thing is that Troilus offers a significant margin of safety, and it should deliver very attractive returns also in the case that the gold price declines somewhere to the $3,000 area.

Another risk is related to the permitting process. Although it is well underway, Troilus often mentions the support of the Quebec government, and it expects the final permits to be issued in H2 2026; anything can happen, and any potential delays may unnerve the investors and lead to meaningful share price declines.

The financing risk must be mentioned too. Although Troilus made a C$172.5 million only recently, it is definitely not enough to finance the mine construction, which should cost around $1.08 billion. Even if Troilus manages to obtain a $1 billion loan, which would be a huge success, further share dilution will be inevitable. There are 40 million warrants outstanding, it is possible to expect a major portion of them to be exercised. Moreover, Troilus will need some buffer to finance its common operations, including exploration activities, until the mine starts generating cash flows. This means at least three years, if everything goes well. Therefore, Troilus will most probably issue another 100-150 million shares, which means an approximately 18-27% share dilution.

And there are also operational risks. The mine needs to be built and ramped up first. It means various risks, including cost overruns and the possibility that something will work less efficiently than projected. However, once again, at the current valuation and current metal prices, Troilus offers a very comfortable margin of safety.

At a gold price of $4,000/toz, the after-tax NPV (5%) of the project is $4.7 billion. At a gold price of $5,000/toz, it is over $6.5 billion. Even if there is the abovementioned 27% share dilution, and the gold price calms down and returns to the $4,000/toz level, the share price will have to grow by more than 350% for the market capitalisation to match the after-tax NPV (5%). Whether the announcement of the financing package, or the obtaining of the final permits, should help to reduce the valuation gap significantly. In other words, if the gold price remains over $4,000/toz, and Troilus secures the financing package and obtains final permits, the share price may grow by 100%-200% before the end of 2026 quite easily.

data.title: 'The Dollar Is Collapsing'... Is It?
data.description:
With panic in the streets in some parts of the country and global relationships in flux, there are many forecasts that the US dollar is no longer the reserve currency it has been for many years. By my analysis, this is all self-interested fluff designed to game politically and personally.

A 40yr+ history of the DXY Index shows the US dollar is not collapsing as these actors claim. There have been many factors for US dollar strength in the past. It has been a haven during stressful times, as during covid and it has been the place for opportunity, as during the lead-up to the internet bubble.

Recently, Trump settled multiple long-term local conflicts, and the shift back to these areas for restoration opportunities is also part of the DXY Index pattern. It should be clearly seen that DXY falls every time EEM and EFA rise, which indicates capital flows are exiting the US dollar for foreign markets.

Note that earlier versions of EEM showing 0.9% growth have been replaced by an MSCI reformatted EMM with less exposure to Chinese issues. This makes the long-term performance of the reformatted EMM look better at 2%, but it does not change my long-term theme that the SP500 outperforms at 10% because of protections to free speech and personal property.

This includes protections for intellectual property. The highly touted Chinese marketplace as a growth theme has been overstated for many years. Communist dictatorships have never been and will never be investment-worthy.

In my opinion, the US dollar is acting as it always has. It fluctuates! There is no collapse as claimed, and no one need be concerned.

data.title: Dolby Laboratories: Solid Q1 Earnings, Valuation Has Improved (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
There has been a brutal sell-off in software stocks over the past few months, with software-as-a-service "SaaS" names taking a particular pounding. This is starting to create some interesting buying opportunities, both for SaaS names directly and also for other adjacent firms.

I'd throw Dolby Laboratories (DLB) in this category. It's not primarily a SaaS business directly, but I believe it has sold off due to a similar underlying bearish thesis. Namely, investors are increasingly worried that advances in artificial intelligence can displace existing software and technology services.

Dolby Labs gets most of its revenues from managing and commercializing a patent library for various audio and video technologies. When tech and consumer products companies want to utilize services such as Dolby Atmos or Dolby Vision, they have to pay the patent owner, Dolby, a licensing fee. Dolby does offer some SaaS services directly as well, such as software for helping video streamers and live event operators.

The bullish case for Dolby has long been that it essentially gets to charge a licensing tax on a wide variety of consumer electronics products along with collecting revenues from other specific use cases such as cinemas, which utilize Dolby's audio and video technologies.

Dolby doesn't have heavy reliance on any one customer or end vertical to drive the business. See this table of its licensing revenues by category. This is a nicely diversified mix:

Meanwhile, profit margins are exceedingly high (88% gross margins), and the business is capital-light. There's also an M&A component here, as Dolby can purchase other firms' patents as well and plug them into its licensing platform. A recent example of that would be Dolby's purchase of the GE Licensing business for $429 million in 2024.

The Bears Make Themselves Heard

While the bull case is intuitively appealing, there is an increasingly popular counterargument now surfacing. When I last wrote about Dolby in July 2025, I made the case that shares were not cheap enough to buy. In that piece, I said:

"I do have some questions about terminal value. To the extent that the business is reliant on collecting royalty checks, there is a risk if people figure out how to bypass your technology and achieve the same results with a different underlying mechanism. Dolby's core audio technologies have proven to have sticking power over the years, but it's not implausible to imagine disruption coming along at some point.

Given the lack of organic top-line growth, Dolby at 19x adjusted earnings and 28x trailing GAAP earnings doesn't look especially cheap for this sort of business, even if it is less expensive than where shares traded on average over the past few years."

This gets to the software sell-off I mentioned above. In recent months, people are really starting to price in perceived AI risk to these sorts of business models. In a world where new coding tools and AI models gain popularity seemingly almost every week, it feels like the moats around existing software is being attacked at unprecedented speed.

It's not much of a stretch to think that Dolby's core audio and video patents could be threatened by AI and faster coding iteration over time. Why wouldn't a smartphone, TV, or stereo equipment company try to code up its own sort of alternative audio and video systems to bypass Dolby's licensing fee?

The bullish counterargument there would be that Dolby's licenses tend to make up a tiny portion of the overall cost of a product. Is a TV or smartphone maker going to risk using some sort of home-cooked and potentially buggy alternative technology to save a small fraction of the total cost of one of their products? Dolby's codecs and software are reliable industry standards that are easy-to-use and widely supported. Many companies and end consumers are willing to pay a tiny fee for convenience and assurance of functionality as opposed to trying to spin up their own alternative.

Regardless of where you fall on the AI vs. existing software and licenses debate, it's clear that the market's perception of Dolby's positioning has greatly eroded:

Over the past year, Dolby has now trailed the S&P 500 by more than 35%. While in July I argued that Dolby stock wasn't pricing in enough terminal value risk, I think the current valuation more suitably accounts for that factor.

Dolby's Earnings Still Sounding Loud And Clear

Dolby has reported earnings three times since my prior article. It announced Q3 earnings at the end of July. In these, EPS of 48 cents beat expectations by 10 cents and revenues of $316 million grew at a 9.3% clip, easily topping consensus. Fast forward to Q4 results, which were released in November, revenue growth slowed to 1%, though this did top expectations. And EPS of 99 cents left the 70 cent analyst consensus figure in the dust.

And on Thursday, Dolby announced its Q1 2026 earnings, with EPS coming in at $1.06, topping expectations by seven cents. Revenues were down 3% year-over-year, but also came in ahead of consensus.

Most importantly, in the Q1 earnings release, Dolby updated its FY' 26 earnings outlook. It raised its midpoint revenue guide slightly to $1.425 billion of revenues in the current fiscal year, which would represent 5% year-over-year growth. Dolby has grown revenues at a 3.3% annualized clip over the past decade, so 2026's projection looks fine compared to its past history.

The company guided to steady margins, with GAAP gross margins set to come in around 88%. Meanwhile, Dolby raised its full-year bottom line guidance to $2.78 of GAAP earnings and $4.37 of non-GAAP earnings for FY'26.

Using non-GAAP earnings, DLB stock is trading at just over 14x forward earnings, and that's using the prior earnings estimates that don't take into account the firm's newly raised guidance yet:

And things get interesting in the out years as analysts expect EPS growth to pick up once again in 2027.

Unfortunately, I'd argue that DLB stock isn't actually as cheap as that above table would indicate. Some of the adjustments to earnings seem reasonable to back out, such as income tax adjustments and restructuring.

However, the majority of the GAAP to non-GAAP adjustments are due to stock-based compensation and amortization of acquisition-related intangible assets. I'm of the view that stock-based comp is a real expense, and investors should consider it accordingly. SBC is easier to cut than cash salaries, and in this tougher job market, I wouldn't be surprised if a company like Dolby can reduce its SBC packages a bit. Still, most of this expense is going to recur year after year and erode total shareholder returns. As for amortization, Dolby buys other patent portfolios to maintain its competitive position, and I consider that to be a form of spending somewhat akin to R&D.

Long story short, backing out SBC and amortization from adjusted earnings, I'd argue that Dolby's real core annualized earnings power is closer to $3/share (slightly above the $2.62 of GAAP earnings it reported in FY'25). On that basis, shares are now approaching 20x earnings with the recent drawdown.

In addition to the falling P/E ratio, DLB stock has come down on other valuation metrics as well. Here's EV/EBITDA for example:

And here is Dolby stock on a price-to-sales basis:

On an EV/EBITDA basis, Dolby is around the middle of its historical range, not looking especially cheap or expensive. On a price-to-sales metric, though, Dolby is starting to look quite attractive, as shares are not that far above where the stock bottomed in the early 2010s.

DLB Stock Verdict

I think AI, and technological progress more generally, is a very real threat to Dolby's core business model. I doubt anything significant changes within the next few years, but a decade or more from now, it's hard to forecast how emerging technologies will disrupt existing software and licensing operations.

That said, ChatGPT was originally released back in 2022. We've been talking about LLMs and AI-enhanced coding for years now. I don't see anything in particular that has happened over the past three months that has suddenly made this a more existential threat to companies like Dolby than it was previously. Is it a real threat? Absolutely. Did it intensify to the point that Dolby shares deserved to underperform the S&P 500 by 30% over the past six months? Probably not.

If Dolby's business were slowing down or showing signs of duress, that would be a different matter. But as discussed above, Dolby's earnings results continue ticking along just fine. The AI-related disruption to the business remains a potential concern rather than anything showing up in the numbers at this time. For investors that find Dolby's high profit margin licensing-based business model interesting, the recent share price decline could mark a solid entry point.

data.title: Coca-Cola: Pricing Power Replaces Volume Growth
data.description:
Coca-Cola (KO) remains one of the most popular brands globally, as mentioned in my last write up that, around 94% of the global population recognises the logo.

The stock hasn't moved much since my last article, as it has underperformed; the S&P 500 continues to outperform, consistent with past performance. As the index is hitting all time highs, Coca-Cola is also now trading near all time highs at the time of writing, around 2% off the all time high. I continue to believe that the opportunity cost for holding Coca-Cola exists as the bull market continues to run, as analysts remain bullish for 2026, such as JP Morgan's 2026 market outlook. I believe next quarter is important for Coca-Cola, as price increases are currently driving growth, rather than volume increases, as Coca-Cola suffers the fate of being 'too popular', as there is no new customer base to expand to. With new disruptors like Celsius, obviously not directly competing with Coca-Cola's core drink, Cola, should continue to be concerning to investors with the shift away from sugary drinks, as though Coca-Cola offers Diet Coke or Coke Zero, might start showing signs of a shift from drinks that taste sugary rather than just containing sugar. With limited upside, I continue to rate Coca-Cola at a 'Hold', remaining one of the best defensive stocks in the market, especially with tariff threats benefitting the stock rather than harming it (January-April 2025).

The Shift from Sugary Drinks

As people become more health conscious about what they eat daily, many products have been modified to fit consumers' tastes, such as decaf coffee or caffeine-free soda, and a key thing in many food products and beverages is sugar.

In the UK, over 7 out of every 10 soft drinks sold are low or no sugar. Volumes of sugary soft drinks in takeaways fell from 2016 to 2025, as the general consensus is a fall in sales of sugary drinks, as companies adapt to more non-sugary drinks for consumers. This is similar in the US, as per capita soft drink consumption has had a long-term downward trend since its 1999 peak.

A key reason for the decline has also been due to sugar taxes/levy, as is common in UK cities like London, where restaurants (McDonald's) would charge slightly more if the consumer buys regular Coca-Cola rather than Diet Coke or Coke Zero. With non-sugary drinks like sugar free energy drink brand Celsius continuing to thrive with revenue growing 172% in the most recent quarter, with Monster Energy known for having extremely high sugar content increasing risk of type 2 diabetes from studies, having over 3x the maximum daily sugar intake for adults in 1 can of Monster, shows how consumers are shifting to more healthier options for their beverages.

From the Q3 earnings call:

And then on the lights and zeros and diets, look, the headline number is zeros and diets are our mid-teens percentage of total soft drink volume.

We called out Diet Coke because I think there was a period of time, decade long, maybe longer, maybe 2 decades, where Diet Coke, particularly in the English-speaking countries, was declining. And it has more recently stabilized over the last years and is actually growing this year as well as seeing Coke Zero Sugar grow

With Coca-Cola's soft drink volume for their Diet Coke and Coke Zero (sugar free versions) being only at around 14%-16% mid-teens, it means that Coca-Cola will be heavily affected if there is a genuine major shift in consumer habits, as Coca-Cola's sugary drinks line hasn't been negatively affected heavily just yet. My main fear is if the sugar tax/levy continues to be a policy across countries, especially for North America, as it is their largest revenue segment. If there is further regulation replicating the UK, Coca-Cola could obviously just pass down the cost to consumers, but any price increases that are not through pricing power (sugar tax price increases wouldn't go to improving their margins) will affect Coca-Cola. I doubt there will be any sudden change in regulation, but with more and more countries having levies on sugar, any country following this levy will affect Coca-Cola, as the room for them to increase prices themselves will fall, as recent stagnant volume doesn't help either, as they have been relying on price/mix increases for revenue growth.

Q3 2025 and Looking out for Q4

My main concern with Coca-Cola is the lack of volume growth, as the growth rate is coming to a period where it will most likely be flat YoY. With volume growth of only 1% YoY, it means that the growth coming from this year majorly comes from price increases. With revenue growth relying on price increases, it will eventually come to a point where price increases are no longer feasible or the price is simply too high for consumers, where they are willing to choose a different product, such as Pepsi's product (also relied on pricing power recently) or simply a local brand. When I discussed Coca-Cola's moat, one of the major weaknesses of Coca-Cola's moat is switching cost, as there is no incentive except for utility to continue to consume Coca-Cola, as the lack of volume growth has started to show signs of a shift in consumer behaviors.

Looking at the results from Q1-Q3 2025, it is still the same case with volume growing 1% YoY, whilst price/mix grew 6%. With higher prices and a more profitable product mix, Coca-Cola's pricing power strategy can really only last so long.

With the only real volume growth being EMEA, which is only 24% of revenue, whilst Latin America, North America, and bottling continue to decline, which is 65% of revenue, it will be one of the key things to look out for in the next earnings report

A key reason for increasing prices tends to be higher COGS (cost of goods sold), but COGS from Q1-Q3 2024 to Q1-Q3 2025 actually fell slightly YoY, as the costs weren't passed down to the consumer, as the costs were actually flat/fell YoY.

From the Q3 earnings call:

We're cycling a more difficult volume comparison in some of our key markets

We're focused on driving balanced top line growth with volume as a key priority

I think as we talked about on the previous call, if our long-term growth model calls for 4% to 6% on the top line and we look for balance, which kind of implies 2% to 3% on volume and 2% to 3% on price,

I believe that volume and price/mix growth is one of the most important aspects to look out for in the next quarter, as a continuous flat growth in volume is negative looking forward for Coca-Cola, as they mention is one of their key priorities looking forward. Coca-Cola is leaning more towards price right now for its top line growth, as improvements in volume are needed to meet its targets. No matter the result, if volume improves or not, the question of volume will definitely (I seriously doubt that it doesn't get asked) get questioned on, as I believe depending on the Q4 result and 2026 outlook, the answer is very important and the view from management on whether volumes will be affected looking forward.

Valuation

I continue to believe that buying Coca-Cola, if not for a dividend portfolio, comes at an opportunity cost, as major indexes like the S&P 500 or a more tech focused index like QQQ will likely outperform Coca-Cola's predictability, unlike big tech's explosive growth and surprises. With Coca-Cola currently trading nearly 50% higher for non-GAAP P/E against the sector median and around similar levels for its historical averages, Coca-Cola isn't exactly undervalued or attractive at these levels. Though PepsiCo trades higher on GAAP P/E, PepsiCo's non-GAAP P/E is around 19, which is lower than Coca-Cola's around 24.5, as Coca-Cola isn't exactly undervalued against PepsiCo, though the GAAP metric makes it seem so.

For a defensive, non-tech and high-dividend play, I believe that Coca-Cola remains one of the top picks for this segment, but with a bullish outlook for 2026 and big tech continuing to impress, as Meta's expectations of $110B capex budget met with Zuckerberg saying their 2026 capex would be $115-$135B, which means that the AI boom has yet to show any signs of slowing down. With tech becoming a larger piece of the S&P 500, it is likely that it will continue to outperform Coca-Cola, but Coca-Cola will outperform during any form of uncertainty, which may rise with recent tariff news starting to escalate again.

Final Thoughts

Coca-Cola is a top choice during times of uncertainty, but I believe it comes at an opportunity cost, where the market is obsessed with big tech and the opportunities that come with it. It could also be argued that Coca-Cola is overvalued, but I don't believe that is exactly the case since Coca-Cola's moat is extremely hard to tumble as it is hard to imagine a world without Coca-Cola even after decades, though disruptions like sugar levies across more countries could be the case. For investors who are slightly worried about valuations and bearish on the market, but still want to collect a solid dividend yield with a stock that doesn't really disappoint, Coca-Cola is a solid pick.

data.title: Camping World Holdings: Is It A Buy With Shares Up 40% YTD?
data.description:
Camping World Holdings (CWH) has certainly seen its bursts and reality checks. In early 2020, shares fell below $5/share only to spike above the $30/share mark by the middle of the pandemic year. The performance has been sporadic since then, with swings higher and lower.

I view the swings as natural, given the seasonal cycles of the RV business. With shares now trading at around the $13.30/share mark and still down 40% over the last year, despite it’s nearly 40% rise YTD, I believe the stock and its outlook is worth an update.

In recent months, the company installed a new CEO and announced a partnership with Costco (COST). While these events could certainly be viewed as forward catalysts, I don’t necessarily believe the valuation is right at this junction in time.

CWH Stock Key Metrics

Despite its significant pullback over the past year, CWH is still a stock that commands a nearly 40x multiple to earnings. Not only is that well above CWH’s historical averages, but it’s a significant premium to the broader markets, which though itself is arguably stretched, is still a better value than CWH.

It could be viewed as a surprise, then, that CWH is still graded highly on valuation by the Seeking Alpha (“SA”) quants. Looking closer, this is due to the company’s low valuation when assessed through the lens of its enterprise value to sales, which at 0.72x is discounted to the sector median of 1.4x.

Still, CWH is viewed negatively overall by the quants, primarily due to its poor grading on profitability and momentum. This echoes the sentiment by the broader SA community, who view CWH as ‘neutral’ as opposed to a downright ‘sell’ by the quants.

The question then becomes ‘what are Wall Street analysts seeing that most others are missing?’ Over the last 90 days, there have been ratings from 12 analysts, and the reports are surprising. 83% of these analysts view the stock as a ‘buy’, with three quarters of those going even further with ‘strong buy’ ratings. These ratings are further accompanied by an average price target north of $17.50/share, representing upside potential of about 30%.

In my view, these ratings are optimistic. I do not necessarily believe CWH’s operating results provide enough of a ‘wow factor’ to justify the implied upside.

CWH Operating Performance

In analyzing CWH’s performance, it’s worth noting the expectation-beating quarter in Q3, reported in late 2025. During the quarter, CWH showed meaningful operational momentum across nearly every key metric.

First, total revenue rose 5% to over +$1.8B, with used RV unit volume jumping more than 30%. This was likely due to the combination of the market environment and the consumer preference for used over new, as well as management’s focus on affordability and value. From a market share perspective, CWH sold nearly 14% of all new and used RVs in North America YOY. This is indicative of its position as the industry’s dominant retailer.

Adjusted EBITDA, meanwhile, surged more than 40% YOY to +$95.7M, driven by higher volumes, better cost discipline, and improving mix. Margins also improved, as SG&A fell 360 basis points as a percentage of gross profit, reflecting ongoing efficiency initiatives and operating leverage.

While new RV pricing remains a headwind, management’s conservative assumptions appear prudent, especially given sequential improvements in new average selling prices and resilient gross profit per unit.

One of the more encouraging aspects of CWH’s results was the company’s balance sheet progress. Net leverage declined by nearly three turns YTD, supported by earnings growth, cash generation, and debt reduction. Furthermore, CWH ended the quarter with +$230M in cash, +$427M of owned used inventory, and nearly +$260M of unencumbered real estate.

CWH Recent Developments & Outlook

CWH entered 2026 with several notable developments, and this could be one factor driving the recent YTD increase.

First, the company announced a leadership transition at the end of 2025. In the release, CWH stated that President Matt Wagner will succeed Marcus Lemonis as CEO, while Lemonis remains closely involved as Co-Founder and Special Advisor. The move makes sense since it will ensure continuity in culture, strategy, and execution. In my view, given the strong Q3 results and accelerating momentum, this transition looks more like a passing of the baton than a change in direction.

Perhaps more noteworthy is CWH’s new partnership with Costco, which the management team views as a meaningful catalyst for new unit demand. The program leverages COST’s massive, high-quality membership base to funnel qualified leads directly into CWH’s inventory, offering transparent, no-haggle pricing and concierge-style service.

In the release, it was noted that early data suggested the leads are skewing toward higher ASP fifth wheel and motorized units. This could ultimately provide a tailwind to revenue and gross profit per unit, in my view, and it is definitely a development to keep an eye on in future earnings releases.

With shares already up nearly 40% YTD and the company guiding toward continued earnings growth and deleveraging, these strategic initiatives could perhaps justify a continued move higher.

Is CWH Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

I, nonetheless, view CWH as a ‘hold’ at current trading levels. Sure, I definitely do not deny that operational progress is underway, nor am I blind to the potential upside from the leadership transition and the Costco partnership. Yes, the company is executing well, gaining market share, improving margins, and aggressively deleveraging the balance sheet. These are all positive developments, and they help explain the stock’s sharp rebound through January.

That said, valuation remains the primary sticking point for me. Even after a steep decline over the past year, shares still trade at a lofty earnings multiple that appears disconnected from the underlying growth profile and cyclical nature of the RV industry. While the Wall Street analysts are broadly optimistic and see meaningful upside toward the mid-to-high teens, I struggle to find a compelling margin of safety at current prices, particularly with broader equity markets near record highs.

Importantly, CWH remains highly sensitive to macro conditions, interest rates, and consumer confidence. Any market pullback, recession concerns, or renewed pressure on discretionary spending could easily create a more attractive entry point, in my view. Given the stock’s historical volatility, patient investors may be rewarded with better opportunities ahead.

Ultimately, I believe CWH is executing well, but after a 40% YTD rally, I don't believe the risk-reward is compelling enough for investment at this time.

data.title: Merion Road Q4 2025 Letter
data.description:
The Long Only strategy was roughly flat during the fourth quarter bringing our 2025 returns to 9.6%. While this is a respectable number on an absolute basis, it lagged the broader market. The elevated returns for the S&P this past year were largely concentrated amongst the largest companies who happen to have meaningful exposure to the AI trade. This dynamic is demonstrated by the fact that the S&P 500, a dollar weighted index, generated a 17.7% return for the year while an equal weighted index (each company in the S&P 500 is given the same allocation) returned 11.2%. Since launching the strategy, I have had a positive mental tilt towards technology; our AI exposure currently sits at about 22% of the portfolio, split amongst GOOG, MSFT, and AMZN. This feels large to me. But when you realize that the Magnificent Seven 1 currently account for 34% of the S&P it means that I am overweight AI relative to the market as a whole.

I feel comfortable where we are at. I do not closet benchmark the index and I do not manage the portfolio to monthly or yearly performance. I am simply trying to outperform the market over the long-term and do so while managing my risks. As I am writing this letter our cash sits at 16% of the portfolio while a relatively safe merger arbitrage position accounts for another 6%.

During the quarter I acquired shares in Janus Henderson Group (JHG) ("JHG") after they received a non-binding acquisition proposal by their largest shareholder to acquire the company at $46.00. With the stock trading below this initial salvo and at a relatively modest premium to its unaffected price, I viewed the risk / return profile to be attractive. I acquired shares over the next few weeks as the market continued to doubt the likelihood of an eventual transaction. A definitive agreement was reached at the end of December to sell the company at a price of $49.00 / share. I continue to own shares as the IRR to close remains attractive.

The Small Cap Fund returned 8.5% during the fourth quarter, bringing our full year returns to 9.2%. After being basically flat through the first 11 months of the year we ended on a strong note. Some of the largest drivers in the quarter were:

  • - Butler National (BUKS). BUKS issued their Q2 report with EBITDA increasing from $6.5m to $8.8m. Importantly, aerospace revenue, margins, and backlog have all moved up meaningfully. The company, management, and board of directors continue to buy stock despite the higher share price.
  • - Bel Fuse (BELFA, BELFB). Last letter I discussed the capital structure arbitrage whereby I bet on the discount of the A & B shares collapsing. In November both classes of equity sold off on their Q3 earnings release, a report that I actually viewed positively. I took that opportunity to cover our shorts thereby positioning us to make money based on both a) overall valuations recovering and b) the B discount shrinking. Since then, both classes of equity have reached all-time highs and the spread currently sits in the single digits.
  • - Undisclosed Short. During the year I put a short position on a stock we had previously been long. The thesis was that management had wasted a boat load of cash on bad acquisitions and that earnings were being masked by accounting gimmicks and questionable add-backs. The stock fell 40% when the company reported their earnings in December and it became apparent that their guided EBITDA was a fantasy.

During the quarter I built a position in Ascent Industries ("ACNT"). ACNT is a small specialty chemicals company that is transforming from an over-levered conglomerate into a pure-play with a pristine balance. In early 2024 the company revamped its management team with the election of a new CEO & CFO, two individuals who have leadership experience working at large cap chemical companies (Dow Chemical) and turnarounds (Clearon – operations improved and business sold). Since then, ACNT has sold off its tubular steel assets, thereby eliminating $70m of debt and leaving the company with almost $60m of cash.

During this time management has been addressing the poor operating performance of its chemical assets. Their first focus was on stabilizing the current operations by raising price on money losing products and walking away from unprofitable business when necessary. From 2023 to the LTM period revenue shrank from $84m to $74m while gross profit improved from $6m to $17m. With that heavy lifting done, the company is turning to organic growth and M&A. Management has indicated that their facilities are currently running at just 50% utilization. In other words, they can bring the company to $150m in revenue with little capital investments. Operating leverage and a shift toward proprietary products should allow the company to grow its EBITDA margins from mid-single digits to >20%. In December, ACNT announced a new program win that is expected to generate more than $10m in annualized revenue at accretive margins. While the market is starting to give the company the benefit of some growth, significant upside remains if they hit their targets.

Below is a chart of our performance by attribution. For the year, the risk-free rate account for +4.0% return, our market exposure added +4.2%, and stock selection accounted for +1.0%. During this period our average beta adjusted net exposure was 48%.

data.title: Curreen Capital Q4 2025 Letter
data.description:
Dear Partner,

Our fund delivered strong results in 2025, finishing up 10.5% in the fourth quarter and 30.97% for the full year. We remained disciplined in our investment strategy, and our portfolio of "ugly ducklings" delivered solid performance.

Oct Nov Dec Q4 2025 YTD Since Inception Annualized
Curreen Capital Partners LP 1.42% 1.63% 7.20% 10.50% 30.97% 301.7% 11.7%
S&P 500 2.34% 0.25% 0.06% 2.66% 17.89% 426.8% 14.1%
MSCI World (US Gross) 2.02% 0.31% 0.83% 3.18% 21.42% 289.6% 11.4%

Our Actions in Q4

During the fourth quarter we sold Havas (at €1.52/share). In reviewing the business, I determined that it does not meet our return on capital requirements. While Havas (HAVSF) is cheap and well run, other businesses are a better fit for our strategy. We bought Fortrea (about 3% points of the fund, at $9.44/share), and about 1% point each of Dye & Durham (DYNDF) (C$6.91/share), GetBusy (£0.77/share), Truecaller (SEK 39.96/share), and VF Corp ($14.25/share).

2025 Performance Review

Curreen Capital's performance over the year was primarily driven by three positions: Fortrea, Siemens Energy (SMEGF), and GetBusy (GETBF).

Fortrea is an example of our "Crazy Cheap" strategy: buying excellent businesses priced as if their current struggles will last forever. These are ugly ducklings that are especially unpopular, and their stock prices have often fallen hard for months. I get very interested when a stock falls below my downside valuation, and as it falls further—specifically, trading at a discount of 33% or more to my downside valuation—I classify the opportunity as "Crazy Cheap".

Fortrea faced headwinds, with weak financial results, the loss of a star CEO, and swirling fears about changing US health policy. "Crazy Cheap" stocks like Fortrea are often going through a painful period, but their low price creates an extremely attractive upside-to-downside. There is still downside risk, but it is dwarfed by the probability and magnitude of the upside. Overall, buying good businesses at "Crazy Cheap" prices has worked well for us.

We bought our position in Siemens Energy in early 2024, anticipating that the end of a decade-long stagnation in global electricity demand would drive improved operating results. After being evenly matched since 2008, increasing efficiency had finally been overcome by increasing usage of electrical devices, and U.S. electricity consumption had resumed making new highs. Since then, the combination of deferred infrastructure maintenance, continued electrification throughout the economy, and the rapid rise of AI has spurred a new cycle of demand. Siemens Energy has been a direct beneficiary of this return to growth.

We added to GetBusy in late 2024 at £0.56/share, a “Crazy Cheap” valuation. GetBusy raised its outlook in September 2025 following the announcement that a large competitor was exiting the market (Thomson Reuters (TRI)' FileCabinet CS). GetBusy's directors and executives repeatedly bought stock at rising prices after the September release. Statistically, this pattern of heavy “insider” buying at rising prices is a positive signal, and the stock remains “Crazy Cheap”.

We Focus on Ugly Ducklings - Especially Spinoffs and The Extremely Cheap

As we look back on the evolution of Curreen Capital since our 2013 launch, our core philosophy remains unchanged. We invest in “ugly ducklings”, which are businesses that meet our three key criteria: a good business, run by a capable manager, bought at an attractive price. We buy good businesses: companies that will earn after-tax returns on capital above 20% over time. We want these businesses to be run by good managers who allocate capital well. We pay an attractive price: buying when the upside-to-downside ratio is at least 5:1.

Since we launched, we have invested primarily in smaller companies - we generally buy businesses with market caps between $300m and $5B. And since we began we have been particularly interested in—and especially successful with—spinoffs. In 2013 our biggest winner was the spinoff Caverion (CAVRF), and in 2025 each of our three big winners came to market as spinoffs. Ugly duckling spinoffs—good businesses, with capable management, selling at an attractive price—continue to be the best place for us to invest.

Outside of spinoffs, “Crazy Cheap” opportunities have presented themselves more frequently over the past three years. I have seen more ugly ducklings sell down to an attractive upside-to-downside, and then drop further, below my downside valuation. These are often companies with weak current operating results, but the businesses are strong and have fine longer-term outlooks. Where we get a discount of 1/3 or more off my downside valuation, I call them “Crazy Cheap” and we buy them.

Previously, it was rare to find quality businesses selling at such low valuations, but there have been plenty over the past few years. As a group, these extremely-cheap ugly ducklings have performed well for us. We will continue to target ugly duckling spinoffs, as well as home in on (non-spinoff) “Crazy Cheap” companies.

Thank you for your continued trust. Finding opportunities for you is a great pleasure, and I am always a phone call away if you have any thoughts or questions.

Sincerely,

Christian Ryther

Appendix

Curreen Capital Investments: all positions larger than 5% of the fund

Advance Auto Parts (AAP)

Advance Auto Parts is a store-based retailer of aftermarket automotive parts and supplies. This includes batteries, windshield wipers and fluid, air filters, motor oil, etc. The company has historically earned decent (approaching 20%) returns on tangible capital. The company is attempting a turnaround, and has fixed its balance sheet and is improving operations. Advance Auto currently trades at an attractive upside-to-downside ratio.

Fortrea (FTRE)

Fortrea helps its biotech and pharmaceutical customers to run clinical trials with the goal of receiving marketing authorization in the U.S. and other countries. Fortrea helps design the trials, recruit investigators and participants, prepare data for regulatory review, and other tasks needed to win the authorization to market new treatments. The company spun out of Labcorp (LH) in June 2023, and is attempting to turn around the business. Fortrea currently trades at an attractive upside-to-downside ratio.

Frontdoor (FTDR)

Frontdoor sells home service plans to homeowners. The company contracts with HVAC and other contractors and dispatches them when customers have problems with one of their major home appliances and systems (furnace, air conditioning, refrigerator, electrical system, etc). Frontdoor spun out of ServiceMaster in October 2018. Frontdoor serves about 2% of U.S. homes, and uses its free cash flow to grow organically, pay down debt, repurchase shares, and acquire complementary businesses.

GetBusy (GETB:GB)

GetBusy provides online document exchange systems—primarily for accountants. GetBusy spun out of Reckon (RCKNF) in August 2017, and has continued to grow since then. GetBusy does not earn money, investing through higher expenses to grow its existing businesses and attempting to launch new products. The underlying businesses are profitable and sustainable in the U.K. and U.S.

Siemens Energy (ENR:GR)

Siemens Energy combines a world-class electrical power generation business, a world-class electricity transmission business, and an onshore and offshore wind turbine business that is attempting a turnaround. The company spun out of Siemens in September 2020. After many years of weak electricity demand in OECD countries, Siemens Energy is benefiting from a cyclical boom in demand.

VF Corp (VFC)

VF Corp manages apparel brands, including The North Face, Timberland, and Vans. The company has fixed its balance sheet, improved capital allocation, and rebuilt its management team in an effort to return the business to growth. I believe that the company has good brands, the skills to manage them well, and a management team that can handle the challenges that the company faces. VF Corp currently trades at an attractive upside-to-downside ratio.

data.title: Philip Morris: Why The Good Times Are Likely To Continue
data.description:
Background

We last covered Philip Morris (PM) in January of 2024 (you can read that article here), and things have gone... let's say, well. Since our last piece, the stock has surged for a total return of 108%, more than doubling the S&P 500's total return of ~48% for the same period.

At the time of our last writing, we noted that the stock faced some headwinds, which we believed were temporary, and that the stock was trading relatively cheaply in comparison to its historic ranges. As we prepare to digest Philip Morris's upcoming earnings (currently slated for Friday, February 6th pre-market), we thought now would be a good time to see if the momentum the stock experienced throughout 2024 and into 2025 is likely to continue.

Let's dive in.

Are The Narratives Intact?

The long-running theme of Philip Morris for the last few years has been the story of the revival of its business largely through its Zyn line of nicotine pouches and its Heated Tobacco Unit [HTU] sales along with the secular decline of its combustible products (i.e., cigarettes). Investors would then be forgiven for taking the last quarter's results with a skeptical eye.

In Q3, the only segment to experience a drop in top-line revenue and incur a $100 million net reduction in operating income was the Americas, but this was driven in the quarter by a promotional 'free can' event, as management referenced in the accompanying conference call.

Investors nervous that the promotional hit might become routine should rest easy--as Philip Morris CFO Emmanuel Babeau put it in the Q3 conference call "the $100 million [hit to the bottom line] is a one-off".

Indeed, management pointed out that the promotion itself was driven to bring back in consumers after a period of restricted supply, supply that we believe will no longer have issues going into Q4 and beyond.

Indeed, the company's most recent volume backs up management's claim and should reassure investors that the promotional hit will not be enduring, as volume sales on nicotine pouches were up 36% year over year.

Of course, the proof will be in the pudding, and we will want to keep an eye out that top-line revenues return to normal while not seeing a deceleration in nicotine pouch adoption and sales.

Corollary to this, we will also keep an eye out on the overall business mix going forward for Philip Morris. One of the prime drivers of HTU and nicotine pouch consumption is the ongoing conversion of smokers and moist snuff (categories that shrank year over year by 3.2% and 7.2%, respectively). This shows up in the company's overall sales mix.

Over the last year, the company has seen its smoke-free segment grow by 3%, and management believes that the runway for further customer conversion and adoption is significant--and we agree.

Taking these things in totality, we believe that yes, the narrative surrounding the future growth prospects for Philip Morris remains alive and well. But... is the stock a good deal?

Valuation

On the valuation front, the story around Philip Morris has evolved significantly. At the time of our last writing, the stock traded in the lower quartile of its 5-year forward P/E and EV/EBITDA range. Today, that's no longer the case.

While no longer trading at the valuation highs it experienced in mid-2025, the stock remains meaningfully re-rated from its 2023 levels and is inarguably more expensive. Of course, this makes perfect sense--during that time the market has recognized Philip Morris as a growth story.

This is, we think, the biggest question facing Philip Morris investors today--can the stock meaningfully re-rate higher from here?

Based on current operating income projections, it is our opinion that the stock is more likely to continue to range than exhibit much further upward momentum. We caveat that with one big if--bets will be off the table if management delivers meaningfully upgraded prospects for the remainder of the year.

For now, however, it appears, though, that the market and buy-side analysts are largely in agreement that the stock is fairly priced at these levels. Of the 18 analysts that cover the stock, there currently exists only a ~2% difference between the stock price as of this writing and analyst average target prices. Even the most bullish analysts have conceded a bit on this front, with the current highest target price dropping from $220 to $200 per share.

Importantly for income-seeking investors, we see no danger to the dividend currently or for the foreseeable future.

As the above chart illustrates, Philip Morris is estimated to continue to pay a generous amount of its EPS to its stock dividend, which, when combined with the company's growth profile, makes the stock quite attractive. In absolute numbers, and to drive home the point, Philip Morris last FY generated $12.2 billion in cash from operations against $8.1 billion in total annual dividend payments.

Risks

With all companies, it is prudent to consider risks above all else, but it remains especially important for companies in industries as litigious as Philip Morris's. We believe that the near-term regulatory picture remains favorable for the company (in the U.S. market at least). Where we believe the company and its investors face the greatest risk, however, is from potential research results that could influence government policy--namely, the potential for the emergence of longer-term, larger studies showing potential health effects of nicotine pouch usage.

The Bottom Line

Today we believe Philip Morris represents a more or less fair value for new entrants into the stock based on our belief that the growth story remains intact. Further, the lifting of the Q3 promotional 'hangover' could provide a near-term tailwind for the stock. For these reasons, we continue to rate Philip Morris a buy.

data.title: Civeo: A Share Cannibalization Story Trading At A Discount
data.description:
Civeo Corporation (CVEO) is not an exciting business in the traditional sense. They run lodges and provide catering for people working in remote areas, mostly in the Australian coal mines and the Canadian oil sands. Because it sits at the intersection of boring hospitality and the cyclical energy sector, it seems that the market will continue to ignore it until the numbers become too loud to dismiss.

With a recent move up to $25 (+7.5% YTD), the fundamentals around the stock are clearly improving. I put this down to the company starting to cannibalise its own share count after activist investor Engine Capital pushed for an overhaul of the capital acquisition strategy. In the third quarter of 2025 alone, they bought back over a million shares. For a company with a float this small, that is a significant statement of intent.

My take is that most investors are still treating Civeo as a low growth camp company and failing to account for the pivot to integrated services in Australia, plus the fact that the share count is disintegrating. If you like businesses that return every spare cent of free cash flow to shareholders while trading at a single digit EBITDA multiple, Civeo might be one of the few clean plays left in the small cap space.

A Tale of Two Geographies

To understand Civeo better, you need to look at its two geographical segments. In Australia, things are going well. This is the growth side of the business. They are benefiting from strong demand in the Bowen Basin (metallurgical coal) and are successfully shifting toward integrated services, i.e. not just building rooms but also managing facilities, food and logistics for sites they don't even own. It's an asset-light model that generates high returns on capital. Management is aiming to hit $500m AUD in revenue from this segment by 2027, and based on the Q3 numbers, they are well on their way.

Then there is Canada. Revenue in this segment was down about 20% YOY in the most recent quarter because of lower activity in the oil sands and the winding down of some big LNG-related work. But even with lower revenue, they managed to grow gross profit. They've leaned into cost-cutting, and margins are actually expanding during a downturn. To me that suggests a disciplined management team that is proactive as opposed to just waiting around for the cycle to turn.

Cash Is The Only Metric That Matters

The Q3-25 results confirmed this. Revenue came in at $170.5m with adj. EBITDA of $28.8m. While the net loss of $0.5m might not screen well, it's mostly noise from depreciation and a few one-off items.

The real story is the free cash flow. Management has committed to using essentially all of their annual FCF to buy back shares. As of Sep-25, they have already returned $52m to shareholders YTD. In the context of a company with a market cap around $290m, that is a huge yield.

Net leverage sits at 2.1x. It's not debt-free, but it's very comfortable for a business with contracted cash flows. They've used a chunk of debt recently to fund some strategic acquisitions in Australia but are already back to paying it down while maintaining the buyback. My view is that as long as leverage stays around 2x, they have a green light to keep retiring shares at these prices.

Where CVEO Should Trade

Management is guiding for 2025 adj EBITDA of roughly $86-91m. Let's take the midpoint of $88.5m. Currently, the EV sits around $480m, so the stock is trading at ~5.4x forward EBITDA. In my view, that multiple is very low for a business growing its most profitable segment (Australia) and that has a dominant position in its legacy markets.

If you look at the broader specialised hospitality and facility space, there's a clear hierarchy. Global giants like Sodexo (SDXAY) are currently screening around 6.2x forward EBITDA, but more specialised service providers like ABM Industries (ABM) command a higher multiple at roughly 7.7x. Considering a gold standard for outsourced catering and support services, e.g. Compass Group (CMPGY), then you're looking at a double digit multiple, often north of 11x.

In my view, Civeo doesn't need to be priced like Compass Group to be a winner. As the Australian segment continues its shift toward asset-light integrated services, a re-rating toward the 7x range, essentially splitting the difference between a Sodexo and an ABM, is entirely reasonable. Based on the midpoint forecast EBITDA, that gets us to a target EV of $619m, i.e. a $38 target price, which equates to a 52% upside from today. That doesn't even account for the shrinking share count nor the potential $500m AUD revenue target in 2027.

What Moves The Needle

The most obvious catalyst for the stock is simply the continuation of the buyback. Every quarter that Civeo reports a lower share count, the EPS potential for the remaining holders moves higher. The pressure from Engine Capital has been the real driver of this narrative. By successfully pushing for the suspension of the dividend in favour of a huge 20% buyback authorisation, the activists have effectively forced management to put a floor under the stock.

Beyond the buyback, I will be keeping an eye on Australian contract wins. They are bidding on several mobile camp projects that should start contributing in H2-26. If they can land one or two of these, the 2026 guidance is likely to surprise to the upside.

There is also the governance factor. With the Nov-25 cooperation agreement bringing two new independent directors to the board, there is now internal accountability to stay the course on capital returns. Whether they eventually look to sell the company is a wildcard, but the current path of share repurchases creates plenty of value on its own.

Risks To The Thesis

It's not all smooth sailing, with the biggest risk being a sharp decline in metallurgical coal prices. In my view, this would cool off the Australian business. While demand for steel (and therefore met coal) remains steady, a global recession would definitely impact occupancy rates.

There is also the risk that the current slump in Canada lasts longer than expected. If the large project delays persist, then the cost-cutting measures might not be sufficient to sustain EBITDA performance. Finally, the weakening Australian dollar may continue to be a headwind by shaving a few more million off the reported topline number.

Bottom Line

Civeo is cheap for a reason, but that reason is starting to evaporate. The Canadian headwinds are being managed and the Australian tailwinds are real. Management is finally acting like owners and aggressively buying back stock.

At $25, you are paying a very low multiple for a business that is essentially printing cash. I don't expect the market to re-rate this to 10x EV/EBITDA overnight but between some modest multiple expansion and the shrinking float, the path to $38 looks very clear to me.
